refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall update the worksite model to reflect current equipment state, environmental conditions, and operational events with a p95 latency not exceeding the customer-defined target (baseline 1.0 s) across all supported update scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-007 requires clarification of scope (what constitutes 'worksite model'), measurement basis (p95 latency metric), and boundary conditions (supported scenarios). High safety criticality demands explicit traceability to safety-critical update types (e.g., collision alerts, operator status changes). Refined FR establishes measurable acceptance criteria and acknowledges customer-specific tailoring.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrumented field trials measuring end-to-end latency from sensor/event origin through model update completion across representative scenarios (teleoperated movement, autonomous task execution, safety event detection). Latency histogram analysis to confirm p95 ≤ target. Traceability matrix linking safety-critical updates to latency requirements.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-007.1: Acquire real-time equipment telemetry (position, orientation, operational state) with sufficient fidelity and frequency to support worksite model updates.
FR-NGHE-007.2: Transmit telemetry and event data from vehicle to worksite model service with bounded network latency.
FR-NGHE-007.3: Process incoming telemetry and events to update worksite model state with deterministic latency.
FR-NGHE-007.4: Distribute updated worksite model to authorized consumers (Site Manager, Tele-operator, Safety Services) with bounded propagation latency.
FR-NGHE-007.5: Prioritize safety-critical updates (collision risk, operator incapacity, emergency stop) to meet stricter latency bounds than routine operational updates.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized worksite model server with direct telemetry ingestion, synchronous update processing, and publish-subscribe distribution to all consumers.
DP-B: Distributed edge-local model replicas on vehicle and tele-operator clients with eventual consistency synchronization and conflict resolution.

DP-C: Hybrid architecture: vehicle maintains local model replica for safety-critical updates (low latency); centralized server maintains authoritative model for operational coordination; asynchronous reconciliation with priority queuing for safety events.
DP-D: Real-time message broker (e.g., MQTT, RabbitMQ) with topic-based routing, latency-aware QoS levels, and consumer-side model reconstruction.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid architecture with local vehicle model replica for safety-critical updates (latency-optimized) and centralized authoritative model for operational coordination, using priority-queued asynchronous reconciliation.

potential_sources: Coupling between vehicle local model and centralized model via reconciliation logic—mitigated by clear ownership (vehicle owns safety-critical state, server owns operational state) and explicit conflict resolution rules.
Coupling between telemetry acquisition (FR-NGHE-007.1) and model update processing (FR-NGHE-007.3)—mitigated by decoupling via message queue.
Coupling between network transport (FR-NGHE-007.2) and model update latency—mitigated by QoS prioritization and fallback to local model for safety-critical decisions.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C avoids tight coupling of all consumers to a single server (DP-A) and avoids eventual consistency risks of fully distributed replicas (DP-B). Hybrid approach aligns with high safety criticality by ensuring safety-critical updates are not delayed by network or server processing bottlenecks.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle has sufficient compute and storage to maintain local model replica. Risk: Embedded systems may have memory constraints; mitigation: define minimal model subset for safety-critical state.
Assumption: Network link supports priority queuing (QoS). Risk: Legacy or constrained networks may not support QoS; mitigation: fallback to time-division multiplexing or dedicated safety channel.

Assumption: Reconciliation logic can resolve conflicts deterministically. Risk: Concurrent updates to same state element may cause divergence; mitigation: implement vector clocks or operational transformation.

Assumption: Latency budget can be partitioned across acquisition, transmission, processing, and distribution. Risk: One component may dominate; mitigation: conduct latency profiling and allocate budget iteratively.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) which state elements are replicated locally vs. centrally, (2) reconciliation strategy (last-write-wins, vector clocks, or application-specific logic), (3) QoS mapping for safety-critical vs. routine updates, (4) fallback behavior if network link degrades.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Top-level system integration boundary encompassing vehicle, teleoperation, and safety services. Owns end-to-end worksite model update latency requirement and orchestrates sub-system interactions.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Equipment state, sensor readings, and operational events flowing from Vehicle to NGHE Solution for model update.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operational commands and model state flowing from NGHE Solution to Vehicle and Teleoperation for execution and situational awareness.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical notifications (collision risk, operator incapacity, emergency stop) flowing from NGHE Solution to Safety Services and authorized operators.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying communication transport (wireless, cellular, or wired) supporting telemetry, tasking, and safety alert exchange with bounded latency and QoS.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level integration boundary. Worksite model update latency is a system-level requirement spanning vehicle, teleoperation, and safety services. Containment is high because the DP (hybrid model architecture) is realized across multiple sub-modules but orchestrated at the NGHE Solution level. Diffusion risk is low if sub-module interfaces are well-defined.

Vehicle owns local model replica and safety-critical update processing (DP-C local component). However, vehicle does not own centralized model, reconciliation logic, or distribution to tele-operator and safety services. Containment is moderate; diffusion risk is significant if reconciliation logic is scattered across vehicle and server.

Teleoperation consumes worksite model updates but does not own model maintenance or latency optimization. Containment is low; diffusion risk is high if tele-operator-specific model caching or filtering logic is not centralized.

Safety Services consumes safety-critical updates (SafetyAlert interface) and may enforce latency bounds for safety-critical state. Containment is moderate; diffusion risk is moderate if safety-specific latency requirements are not clearly separated from operational latency.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: NGHE Solution (system-level integration) with delegated ownership of sub-components: Vehicle owns local model replica and safety-critical update processing; a dedicated Worksite Model Service (within NGHE Solution) owns centralized model and reconciliation logic.
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Worksite model update latency can be achieved by: (1) Vehicle maintaining local model replica with low-latency safety-critical update processing (owned by Vehicle), (2) NGHE Solution operating a centralized Worksite Model Service that ingests telemetry via Telemetry interface, processes updates, and distributes via Tasking and SafetyAlert interfaces, (3) Network Link providing QoS-aware transport with priority queuing for safety-critical updates. Containment is feasible if sub-module interfaces are well-defined and latency budgets are allocated.
containment_risks: Risk: Reconciliation logic between vehicle local model and centralized model may not be contained within a single module, leading to diffusion across vehicle firmware and server code. Mitigation: Define explicit reconciliation protocol and centralize conflict resolution in Worksite Model Service.

Risk: Latency optimization may require cross-cutting concerns (e.g., network QoS, database indexing, caching) that span multiple modules. Mitigation: Establish latency budget allocation and performance monitoring across module boundaries.

Risk: Safety-critical update prioritization may require changes to Network Link, Vehicle, and Safety Services simultaneously. Mitigation: Define safety-critical update types and QoS mappings at NGHE Solution level; implement as configuration rather than code changes.

Risk: Tele-operator and Site Manager may have different latency requirements for the same model state. Mitigation: Implement consumer-specific filtering and caching within Teleoperation and Safety Services; define SLAs per consumer.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine IM assignment to explicitly define: (1) Worksite Model Service as a dedicated sub-module within NGHE Solution responsible for centralized model maintenance and reconciliation, (2) latency budget allocation across Vehicle (local update processing), Network Link (transmission), and Worksite Model Service (processing and distribution), (3) interface contracts for Telemetry (schema, frequency, QoS), Tasking (command latency SLA), SafetyAlert (priority levels and delivery guarantees), and Network Link (QoS classes and fallback behavior).

fr_dp_gate.reason: Refined FR clearly specifies measurable acceptance criteria (p95 latency ≤ customer target), scope (worksite model update), and safety criticality. Selected DP-C (hybrid architecture) directly addresses the latency requirement by decoupling safety-critical updates (low latency via local vehicle model) from operational updates (eventual consistency via centralized server). DP-C is traceable to FR and avoids over-specification of implementation details (e.g., specific database technology).

diffusion_risks: Reconciliation logic between vehicle and server may diffuse across multiple modules if not explicitly owned. Mitigation: Assign reconciliation to Worksite Model Service within NGHE Solution.

Latency optimization (caching, indexing, QoS tuning) may require changes across Vehicle, Network Link, and Worksite Model Service. Mitigation: Establish latency budget allocation and performance monitoring framework at NGHE Solution level.

Safety-critical update prioritization may require coordination across Vehicle (telemetry acquisition), Network Link (QoS), and Safety Services (alert distribution). Mitigation: Define safety-critical update types and priority mappings at NGHE Solution level; implement as configuration.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for system-level latency orchestration. Sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are well-defined and have clear ownership of their respective components of the DP. Network Link is a known interface supporting QoS-aware transport. Containment is feasible if sub-module interfaces are contractually defined (latency SLAs, message schemas, priority levels).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Worksite model update latency can be verified via instrumented field trials measuring end-to-end latency across representative scenarios. Latency histogram analysis can confirm p95 ≤ target. Traceability matrix can link safety-critical updates to latency requirements. Verification is feasible within NGHE Solution boundary.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Latency requirement is allocated to sub-FRs (telemetry acquisition, transmission, processing, distribution) and sub-modules (Vehicle, Network Link, Worksite Model Service). Allocation is clear and traceable. Safety-critical updates are explicitly prioritized. Allocation clarity is high.

recommended_revisions: Refine sub-FR-NGHE-007.5 to explicitly define safety-critical update types (e.g., collision risk, operator incapacity, emergency stop) and their latency bounds (e.g., p95 ≤ 100 ms for collision alerts).
Define latency budget allocation across sub-FRs: e.g., telemetry acquisition ≤ 100 ms, transmission ≤ 200 ms, processing ≤ 400 ms, distribution ≤ 300 ms (total p95 ≤ 1.0 s).
Specify Network Link QoS requirements: e.g., priority queuing for safety-critical updates, bandwidth reservation, fallback to lower-latency channel if primary link degrades.
Define reconciliation protocol for vehicle local model and centralized model: e.g., vector clocks, last-write-wins with timestamp, or application-specific conflict resolution.
Establish performance monitoring and alerting: e.g., latency SLA violations trigger investigation and corrective action.

decision.justification: Refined FR-NGHE-007 is decomposable into five sub-FRs (telemetry acquisition, transmission, processing, distribution, and safety-critical prioritization). Selected DP-C (hybrid architecture) is sound and addresses latency and safety criticality. Assigned IM (NGHE Solution with sub-modules) is appropriate for system-level integration. However, sub-FRs require further decomposition to allocate latency budgets, define safety-critical update types, and specify interface contracts. Proceeding to next decomposition level is necessary to establish detailed design and verification criteria.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-007.1: Acquire real-time equipment telemetry (position, orientation, operational state) with sufficient fidelity and frequency to support worksite model updates.
FR-NGHE-007.2: Transmit telemetry and event data from vehicle to worksite model service with bounded network latency.
FR-NGHE-007.3: Process incoming telemetry and events to update worksite model state with deterministic latency.
FR-NGHE-007.4: Distribute updated worksite model to authorized consumers (Site Manager, Tele-operator, Safety Services) with bounded propagation latency.
FR-NGHE-007.5: Prioritize safety-critical updates (collision risk, operator incapacity, emergency stop) to meet stricter latency bounds than routine operational updates.

assumptions: Vehicle has sufficient compute and storage to maintain local model replica for safety-critical state.
Network link supports QoS-aware transport with priority queuing.
Reconciliation logic can resolve conflicts between vehicle local model and centralized model deterministically.
Latency budget can be partitioned across acquisition, transmission, processing, and distribution.
Safety-critical update types are well-defined and can be prioritized independently of operational updates.
Customer-defined latency target is achievable with hybrid architecture (DP-C) and available technology.
open_risks: Reconciliation logic may not be contained within a single module, leading to diffusion across vehicle firmware and server code.
Latency optimization may require cross-cutting concerns (network QoS, database indexing, caching) that span multiple modules.
Safety-critical update prioritization may require simultaneous changes to Vehicle, Network Link, and Safety Services.
Tele-operator and Site Manager may have conflicting latency requirements for the same model state.
Network link degradation (e.g., packet loss, increased latency) may violate latency SLA; fallback behavior is not yet defined.
Worksite model size and update frequency may grow beyond vehicle local storage or network bandwidth capacity.
evidence_needed: Latency budget allocation across sub-FRs (telemetry acquisition, transmission, processing, distribution).
Definition of safety-critical update types and their latency bounds.
Network Link QoS requirements and fallback behavior.
Reconciliation protocol for vehicle local model and centralized model.
Field trial results confirming p95 latency ≤ customer target across representative scenarios.
Traceability matrix linking safety-critical updates to latency requirements and verification methods.
